input.topic: How commercial cleaning companies can win facilities manager contracts
input.coreInsight: Facilities managers shortlist cleaning contractors based on responsiveness and specificity — generic outreach gets ignored. A 3-email sequence that references their industry, building size, and cleaning frequency converts at 3–5x the rate of a generic pitch.
input.targetAudience: Facilities managers in offices, healthcare, and education

linkedInPosts.hook: Facilities managers don't ignore bad cleaning companies. They ignore bad emails.
linkedInPosts.body:
Most commercial cleaning pitches land in the bin within 3 seconds.

Not because the company isn't good enough.
Because the email reads like it was written for anyone.

Here's what I see constantly:
→ "We provide high-quality cleaning services to businesses of all sizes."
→ "Our trained team delivers consistent results."
→ "We'd love the opportunity to quote."

A facilities manager at a 40,000 sq ft NHS-approved office block reads that and thinks: this person knows nothing about my building, my compliance requirements, or my current headaches.

Here's what actually gets a reply:

A cold email that mentions:
— Their industry (healthcare, education, commercial offices)
— Their likely building size or site type
— The specific cleaning frequency that fits their operation
— One relevant pain point (contractor reliability, audit readiness, last-minute cover)

That's it. Four lines of specificity beats four paragraphs of credentials every time.

Facilities managers shortlist on responsiveness and relevance. Generic outreach signals neither.

linkedInPosts.hook: 6 reasons your cold emails to facilities managers aren't converting (and how to fix each one):
linkedInPosts.body:
1. You're leading with your company, not their problem.
FMs don't care who you are yet. Open with something they recognise: "Most FMs managing multi-floor office blocks tell us contractor reliability is their biggest headache."

2. Your subject line is invisible.
"Cleaning Services Quote" is deleted on autopilot. Try: "Cleaning cover for [Building Type] in [City]" — it reads like a conversation, not a pitch.

3. You're sending one email and giving up.
FMs are busy. A 3-email sequence — intro, follow-up, final check-in — converts at 3–5x the rate of a single send.

4. You're not referencing their industry.
Healthcare cleaning has compliance angles. Schools have term-time schedules. Offices have out-of-hours requirements. Show you understand their world.

5. You're asking for too much too soon.
Don't lead with "Can we arrange a site visit?" Lead with "Would a quick 10-minute call make sense?" — lower friction, higher response rate.

6. Your email has no specificity.
Building size. Cleaning frequency. Sector-relevant pain point. Three details that turn a cold email into a relevant one.

Facilities managers shortlist contractors who feel prepared before they've even met them.

linkedInPosts.hook: She managed a 12-site healthcare portfolio and hadn't changed her cleaning contractor in 4 years — until one email landed differently.
linkedInPosts.body:
Sarah is a facilities manager for a private healthcare group.

12 sites. Tight compliance requirements. Zero tolerance for missed cleans or contractor no-shows.

She gets cold outreach from cleaning companies every week.

Every single one says something like:
"We're a professional cleaning company with experienced staff and competitive rates."

She deletes them all.

Then one Tuesday morning, an email arrives. Subject line: "Healthcare cleaning for multi-site operations in the East Midlands."

It opens with:
"Managing cleaning compliance across multiple healthcare sites usually means one of two problems — inconsistent standards between locations, or contractors who can't scale when you need extra cover."

She stops scrolling.

The email goes on to reference CQC environment standards, outlines a proposed weekly frequency for clinical and non-clinical areas, and asks one simple question: "Is this something worth a 10-minute call?"

She replies within the hour.

The cleaning company had never worked with her before. They didn't have a referral. They had no existing relationship.

They just did one thing differently:

They wrote an email that made Sarah feel understood before she'd ever met them.

Specificity is your competitive advantage. Most of your competitors won't bother.

linkedInPosts.hook: Stop asking facilities managers for a site visit. It's killing your conversion rate.
linkedInPosts.body:
Every cold email guide for cleaning companies says the same thing:

"End with a call to action — ask for a site visit."

It sounds logical. You need to see the building. They need to see you're serious.

But here's the problem:

A site visit is a big ask from a stranger.

You're asking a busy facilities manager — who receives cold outreach weekly — to commit 30–60 minutes of their day to someone they've never spoken to, based on a single email they haven't fully read yet.

The friction is too high. They don't reply.

Not because they're not interested. Because you moved too fast.

The cleaning companies winning FM contracts through cold email are doing the opposite:

They ask for a 10-minute call first.

That's it. One small, low-commitment step that says: "I'm not here to waste your time."

Once you're on the call, you earn the site visit.
Once you're on-site, you earn the quote.
Once you quote well, you win the contract.

The companies trying to skip straight to a site visit are losing to the companies who understand that trust is built in stages.

Reduce the ask. Increase the response.

linkedInPosts.hook: How to write a 3-email cold sequence that gets facilities managers to reply (step by step):
linkedInPosts.body:
Most cleaning companies send one generic email and wonder why no one responds.

Here's the exact structure that converts at 3–5x that rate:

---

📧 EMAIL 1 — The Relevant Intro (Day 1)

Subject: [Sector] cleaning for [Building Type] in [City]

Open with their pain, not your pitch.
Example: "Most facilities managers overseeing office buildings in [City] tell us their biggest frustration is contractors who go quiet after the first month."

Then:
— One line on who you are
— Reference their building type and likely cleaning frequency
— One soft CTA: "Worth a 10-minute call this week?"

Keep it under 120 words.

---

📧 EMAIL 2 — The Follow-Up (Day 4–5)

Don't resend the first email. Add value.

Reference something sector-specific:
"We've recently helped [similar building type] in [City] reduce cleaning gaps during peak hours — happy to share what worked if it's useful."

Same CTA. Keep it short.

---

📧 EMAIL 3 — The Close (Day 10–12)

Be direct. This is your last touch.

"I won't keep following up — but if regular commercial cleaning cover for [building/site] ever becomes a priority, we'd be ready to move quickly."

Leave the door open. No pressure. No apology.

---

Three emails. Plain text. Sector-specific language.

That's the sequence. The results come from the specificity, not the volume.

blogArticle.title: How to Win Facilities Manager Contracts with Cold Email (3-Sequence Strategy)

seo.primaryKeyword: how to win facilities manager contracts
secondaryKeywords: cold email for commercial cleaning companies
how to get commercial cleaning clients
seo.metaTitle: Win Facilities Manager Contracts with Cold Email
seo.metaDescription: Learn how a 3-email cold outreach sequence helps commercial cleaning companies win facilities manager contracts in offices, healthcare, and schools.
internalLinkSuggestions: How to Build a Commercial Cleaning Prospect List That Actually Converts
LinkedIn Outreach for Commercial Cleaning Companies: A Step-by-Step Guide
How to Price Commercial Cleaning Contracts to Win Tenders
localSeoVariations: how to win facilities manager contracts in London
commercial cleaning contracts Manchester facilities managers
facilities manager cold email Birmingham commercial cleaning

blogArticle.introduction:
Most commercial cleaning companies send the same generic pitch to every facilities manager on their list. 'We offer high-quality cleaning services at competitive rates.' It goes straight to the bin — and so does the next follow-up.

Facilities managers are busy. They manage multi-site buildings, juggle contractors, and have no patience for outreach that wastes their time. If your cold email doesn't reference their industry, their building type, or their specific cleaning challenges, you're invisible.

This article shows you exactly how to build a 3-email cold outreach sequence that gets facilities managers to respond. You'll learn why specificity beats volume every time, what to say in each email, which mistakes kill your chances before the first reply, and which tools make the process repeatable at scale.

This is for cleaning company owners who are serious about winning recurring commercial contracts — not one-off jobs, but long-term agreements with offices, healthcare sites, schools, and managed facilities.

sections.heading: Why Facilities Managers Ignore Most Cleaning Outreach
sections.content:
Facilities managers are among the most targeted prospects in the B2B service sector. They receive outreach from cleaning companies, waste contractors, maintenance firms, and security providers every single week. Generic emails don't stand out — they confirm you haven't done your homework.

Here's the reality: a facilities manager responsible for a 40,000 sq ft NHS-leased office building has entirely different concerns from one managing a chain of secondary schools. Their cleaning frequency requirements differ. Their compliance obligations differ. Their sign-off processes differ.

When your outreach treats both of them the same, it signals that you're not the kind of contractor who pays attention to detail. And in commercial cleaning, attention to detail is literally what you're being hired to demonstrate.

**The core issue isn't volume — it's relevance.** Cleaning companies that win facilities manager contracts consistently aren't sending more emails. They're sending better ones. Targeted, specific, and timed to land when a contract is likely up for review.

Facilities managers shortlist contractors based on two things: responsiveness and specificity. Your cold email is your first test on both counts.

sections.heading: The 3-Email Sequence That Converts at 3–5x the Industry Average
sections.content:
A well-structured cold email sequence doesn't try to close a deal in the first message. It builds familiarity, demonstrates expertise, and makes it easy for the facilities manager to take one small step forward. Here's how to structure all three emails.

### Email 1: The Specific Introduction

Your first email has one job: prove you're not sending a mass blast.

Reference their sector, their building type, or a known challenge in their industry. Keep it under 150 words. No attachments. No pricing. No case study wall of text.

**Subject line formula:** [Their industry] + [specific cleaning challenge] + [your result]
Example: *'Healthcare contract cleaning in [City] — how we cut complaint rates for NHS facilities managers'

**Body structure:**
- One sentence that references their specific context (sector, building size, or a compliance requirement like COSHH or CQC)
- One sentence on a problem you solve that's relevant to their role
- One sentence on a result you've delivered for a similar client (no client name needed)
- A single low-friction CTA: 'Would it be worth a 10-minute call this week?'

Don't ask them to fill in a form, read a brochure, or visit your website. Just ask for a call.

### Email 2: The Value-Add Follow-Up (Day 4–5)

If no reply, send a follow-up that adds something useful — not a repeat of email one.

This is where you demonstrate sector knowledge. Include a brief, specific insight relevant to their industry. For example, if you're targeting education facilities managers, reference the DfE cleaning standards for schools, or common issues during term changeovers. If it's healthcare, reference infection control cleaning protocols.

**Subject line formula:** A direct reference to the insight
Example: *'One thing most school cleaning contracts miss during term breaks'

Keep it under 120 words. End with the same low-friction CTA from email one. You're not chasing — you're adding value on your way past.

### Email 3: The Permission-Based Close (Day 9–11)

If still no reply, send a short, honest final email. This is your 'breakup' email — and it consistently gets the highest reply rate of the three.

Acknowledge that timing might be off. Give them an easy way to opt out or to re-engage when their current contract is up for review.

**Example closing line:** *'If your current contract isn't up for review until next year, just let me know and I'll follow up then — no hard feelings either way.'

This removes pressure and often triggers a reply from facilities managers who were interested but too busy. Many will give you a contract renewal date — which is gold.

**Sequence timing summary:**
- Email 1: Day 0
- Email 2: Day 4–5
- Email 3: Day 9–11

Do not email more than three times in an initial sequence. Beyond that, move to a quarterly re-engagement cadence.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes That Kill Your Outreach Before It Starts
sections.content:
Even cleaning companies with a good service proposition regularly sabotage their own cold outreach. Here are the mistakes that account for most failed campaigns.

**Mistake 1: Leading with price or a discount**
Opening with 'competitive rates' or 'free first clean' signals desperation, not competence. Facilities managers are not looking for the cheapest contractor — they're looking for the most reliable one. Price-led outreach attracts price-led clients who will leave the moment a cheaper quote arrives.

**Mistake 2: Sending from a free email domain**
Emails sent from Gmail or Outlook personal addresses are filtered as spam or dismissed as unprofessional. Your cold email must come from a domain-matched business address (e.g. james@apexcleaningservices.co.uk). This alone improves deliverability significantly.

**Mistake 3: Targeting the wrong person**
In larger organisations, facilities managers may not be the contract decision-maker — that could be a procurement lead, a head of operations, or an estates director. Verify the correct contact before you build your sequence. LinkedIn is your primary tool for this.

**Mistake 4: Using one generic template for all sectors**
An email written for a property management company will not resonate with an NHS trust facilities manager. The compliance language is different, the operational pressures are different, and the contract structure is different. Segment your list by sector and write a distinct email template for each.

**Mistake 5: No follow-up system**
The majority of commercial cleaning contracts are won on the second or third touchpoint. Cleaning companies that send one email and wait have no pipeline — they have hope. A CRM or email sequencing tool is not optional if you want predictable contract wins.

sections.heading: Tools to Build and Automate Your Outreach Pipeline
sections.content:
You don't need a large team or a significant budget to run effective cold outreach for commercial cleaning contracts. You need the right tools and a consistent process.

### Prospect List Building

**LinkedIn Sales Navigator** – The most reliable tool for identifying facilities managers by industry, company size, and location. Filter by job title (Facilities Manager, Estates Manager, Head of Operations) and geography. Export to a spreadsheet or CRM.

**Apollo.io** – Useful for finding verified email addresses at scale, particularly for mid-size commercial organisations. Cross-reference with LinkedIn to confirm the contact is current.

**Companies House + Google Maps** – For local commercial targeting, search business parks, healthcare sites, and school trusts in your area. Cross-reference with LinkedIn to find the facilities or operations contact.

### Email Sequencing and Tracking

**Instantly.ai or Lemlist** – Both allow you to build automated multi-step sequences with personalisation fields (industry, building type, location). Track open rates, reply rates, and clicks per sequence so you know which templates are performing.

**Google Workspace (domain email)** – Essential for deliverability. If you're not emailing from your own domain, fix this before you run a single sequence.

### CRM for Pipeline Management

**HubSpot Free CRM or GoHighLevel** – Track every prospect, log replies, and set follow-up reminders for facilities managers who gave you a future contract review date. A pipeline without a CRM is a list you'll forget to work.

### Benchmarks to Aim For

A well-targeted cold email sequence for commercial cleaning should achieve:
- **Open rate:** 35–50% (with a strong subject line and clean domain)
- **Reply rate:** 5–12% (with a specific, sector-relevant sequence)
- **Booked calls per 100 prospects:** 3–8

If your numbers are below this, the issue is usually targeting (wrong contacts), relevance (wrong message), or deliverability (wrong sending setup).

blogArticle.conclusion:
Winning facilities manager contracts through cold email is not about sending more emails. It's about sending the right message to the right person at the right time — and following up with discipline.

The 3-email sequence in this article works because it treats facilities managers as the busy, informed professionals they are. It references their world, demonstrates your expertise, and asks for something small. That's what earns a reply.

Start by segmenting your prospect list by sector. Build one tailored sequence per sector. Set up your domain email and sequencing tool. Then run your first 50-contact campaign and track what's working.

If you want to skip the trial and error and build a cold outreach system that's already been tested across commercial cleaning companies, book a free strategy call with the CleanReach team. We'll audit your current outreach, identify the gaps, and map out a sequence built for your target sectors.
blogArticle.fullMarkdown:
# How to Win Facilities Manager Contracts with Cold Email (3-Sequence Strategy)

Most commercial cleaning companies send the same generic pitch to every facilities manager on their list. *'We offer high-quality cleaning services at competitive rates.'* It goes straight to the bin — and so does the next follow-up.

Facilities managers are busy. They manage multi-site buildings, juggle contractors, and have no patience for outreach that wastes their time. If your cold email doesn't reference their industry, their building type, or their specific cleaning challenges, you're invisible.

This article shows you exactly how to build a 3-email cold outreach sequence that gets facilities managers to respond. You'll learn why specificity beats volume every time, what to say in each email, which mistakes kill your chances before the first reply, and which tools make the process repeatable at scale.

This is for cleaning company owners who are serious about winning recurring commercial contracts — not one-off jobs, but long-term agreements with offices, healthcare sites, schools, and managed facilities.

---

## Why Facilities Managers Ignore Most Cleaning Outreach

Facilities managers are among the most targeted prospects in the B2B service sector. They receive outreach from cleaning companies, waste contractors, maintenance firms, and security providers every single week. Generic emails don't stand out — they confirm you haven't done your homework.

Here's the reality: a facilities manager responsible for a 40,000 sq ft NHS-leased office building has entirely different concerns from one managing a chain of secondary schools. Their cleaning frequency requirements differ. Their compliance obligations differ. Their sign-off processes differ.

When your outreach treats both of them the same, it signals that you're not the kind of contractor who pays attention to detail. And in commercial cleaning, attention to detail is literally what you're being hired to demonstrate.

**The core issue isn't volume — it's relevance.** Cleaning companies that win facilities manager contracts consistently aren't sending more emails. They're sending better ones. Targeted, specific, and timed to land when a contract is likely up for review.

Facilities managers shortlist contractors based on two things: responsiveness and specificity. Your cold email is your first test on both counts.

---

## The 3-Email Sequence That Converts at 3–5x the Industry Average

A well-structured cold email sequence doesn't try to close a deal in the first message. It builds familiarity, demonstrates expertise, and makes it easy for the facilities manager to take one small step forward. Here's how to structure all three emails.

### Email 1: The Specific Introduction

Your first email has one job: prove you're not sending a mass blast.

Reference their sector, their building type, or a known challenge in their industry. Keep it under 150 words. No attachments. No pricing. No case study wall of text.

**Subject line formula:** [Their industry] + [specific cleaning challenge] + [your result]

Example: *'Healthcare contract cleaning in [City] — how we cut complaint rates for NHS facilities managers'*

**Body structure:**
- One sentence that references their specific context (sector, building size, or a compliance requirement like COSHH or CQC)
- One sentence on a problem you solve that's relevant to their role
- One sentence on a result you've delivered for a similar client (no client name needed)
- A single low-friction CTA: *'Would it be worth a 10-minute call this week?'*

Don't ask them to fill in a form, read a brochure, or visit your website. Just ask for a call.

### Email 2: The Value-Add Follow-Up (Day 4–5)

If no reply, send a follow-up that adds something useful — not a repeat of email one.

This is where you demonstrate sector knowledge. Include a brief, specific insight relevant to their industry. For example, if you're targeting education facilities managers, reference the DfE cleaning standards for schools, or common issues during term changeovers. If it's healthcare, reference infection control cleaning protocols.

**Subject line formula:** A direct reference to the insight

Example: *'One thing most school cleaning contracts miss during term breaks'*

Keep it under 120 words. End with the same low-friction CTA from email one. You're not chasing — you're adding value on your way past.

### Email 3: The Permission-Based Close (Day 9–11)

If still no reply, send a short, honest final email. This is your 'breakup' email — and it consistently gets the highest reply rate of the three.

Acknowledge that timing might be off. Give them an easy way to opt out or to re-engage when their current contract is up for review.

**Example closing line:** *'If your current contract isn't up for review until next year, just let me know and I'll follow up then — no hard feelings either way.'*

This removes pressure and often triggers a reply from facilities managers who were interested but too busy. Many will give you a contract renewal date — which is gold.

**Sequence timing summary:**
- Email 1: Day 0
- Email 2: Day 4–5
- Email 3: Day 9–11

Do not email more than three times in an initial sequence. Beyond that, move to a quarterly re-engagement cadence.

---

## Common Mistakes That Kill Your Outreach Before It Starts

Even cleaning companies with a good service proposition regularly sabotage their own cold outreach. Here are the mistakes that account for most failed campaigns.

**Mistake 1: Leading with price or a discount**

Opening with 'competitive rates' or 'free first clean' signals desperation, not competence. Facilities managers are not looking for the cheapest contractor — they're looking for the most reliable one. Price-led outreach attracts price-led clients who will leave the moment a cheaper quote arrives.

**Mistake 2: Sending from a free email domain**

Emails sent from Gmail or Outlook personal addresses are filtered as spam or dismissed as unprofessional. Your cold email must come from a domain-matched business address (e.g. james@apexcleaningservices.co.uk). This alone improves deliverability significantly.

**Mistake 3: Targeting the wrong person**

In larger organisations, facilities managers may not be the contract decision-maker — that could be a procurement lead, a head of operations, or an estates director. Verify the correct contact before you build your sequence. LinkedIn is your primary tool for this.

**Mistake 4: Using one generic template for all sectors**

An email written for a property management company will not resonate with an NHS trust facilities manager. The compliance language is different, the operational pressures are different, and the contract structure is different. Segment your list by sector and write a distinct email template for each.

**Mistake 5: No follow-up system**

The majority of commercial cleaning contracts are won on the second or third touchpoint. Cleaning companies that send one email and wait have no pipeline — they have hope. A CRM or email sequencing tool is not optional if you want predictable contract wins.

---

## Tools to Build and Automate Your Outreach Pipeline

You don't need a large team or a significant budget to run effective cold outreach for commercial cleaning contracts. You need the right tools and a consistent process.

### Prospect List Building

**LinkedIn Sales Navigator** – The most reliable tool for identifying facilities managers by industry, company size, and location. Filter by job title (Facilities Manager, Estates Manager, Head of Operations) and geography. Export to a spreadsheet or CRM.

**Apollo.io** – Useful for finding verified email addresses at scale, particularly for mid-size commercial organisations. Cross-reference with LinkedIn to confirm the contact is current.

**Companies House + Google Maps** – For local commercial targeting, search business parks, healthcare sites, and school trusts in your area. Cross-reference with LinkedIn to find the facilities or operations contact.

### Email Sequencing and Tracking

**Instantly.ai or Lemlist** – Both allow you to build automated multi-step sequences with personalisation fields (industry, building type, location). Track open rates, reply rates, and clicks per sequence so you know which templates are performing.

**Google Workspace (domain email)** – Essential for deliverability. If you're not emailing from your own domain, fix this before you run a single sequence.

### CRM for Pipeline Management

**HubSpot Free CRM or GoHighLevel** – Track every prospect, log replies, and set follow-up reminders for facilities managers who gave you a future contract review date. A pipeline without a CRM is a list you'll forget to work.

### Benchmarks to Aim For

A well-targeted cold email sequence for commercial cleaning should achieve:

- **Open rate:** 35–50% (with a strong subject line and clean domain)
- **Reply rate:** 5–12% (with a specific, sector-relevant sequence)
- **Booked calls per 100 prospects:** 3–8

If your numbers are below this, the issue is usually targeting (wrong contacts), relevance (wrong message), or deliverability (wrong sending setup).

---

## Start Winning Facilities Manager Contracts This Quarter

Winning facilities manager contracts through cold email is not about sending more emails. It's about sending the right message to the right person at the right time — and following up with discipline.

The 3-email sequence in this article works because it treats facilities managers as the busy, informed professionals they are. It references their world, demonstrates your expertise, and asks for something small. That's what earns a reply.

Start by segmenting your prospect list by sector. Build one tailored sequence per sector. Set up your domain email and sequencing tool. Then run your first 50-contact campaign and track what's working.

If you want to skip the trial and error and build a cold outreach system that's already been tested across commercial cleaning companies, book a free strategy call with the CleanReach team. We'll audit your current outreach, identify the gaps, and map out a sequence built for your target sectors.

---

**📅 [Book your free cold email strategy call with CleanReach](https://api.leadconnectorhq.com/widget/bookings/call-with-cleanreach)**

No hard sell. Just a focused 30-minute call on how to build a cold outreach pipeline that wins commercial cleaning contracts.

emailNewsletter.subjectLine: Why FMs ignore your cold emails
emailNewsletter.previewText: It's not your price. It's that your email could've been sent to anyone.
emailNewsletter.body:
Most cold emails to facilities managers get deleted in under three seconds. Not because the timing was wrong. Because the email read like it was written for no one in particular.

Facilities managers — in offices, healthcare, and education especially — are responsible for keeping complex buildings running. They get pitched constantly. Generic outreach like "we offer high-quality cleaning services at competitive rates" tells them nothing about whether you understand their environment.

Here's what actually works: a 3-email sequence built around specificity.

Email one references their industry and the cleaning challenges specific to it. A healthcare FM worries about infection control compliance. An education FM worries about turnaround time between terms. Lead with that, not your company history.

Email two mentions their building size or likely cleaning frequency — showing you've done basic research. Something like: "For a facility your size, most of our clients run a 5-day schedule with monthly deep cleans. Happy to share what that looks like if it's useful." That one line separates you from 90% of your competition.

Email three is a short, direct follow-up. No guilt. No "just circling back." Just a low-friction ask: a 10-minute call or a site visit offer. Keep it one sentence.

This sequence consistently converts at 3–5x the rate of a generic pitch — because it signals that you understand their building before they've said a word to you.

Facilities managers shortlist contractors who seem prepared. Your cold email is your first impression of how you'd operate on-site. Make it specific, make it relevant, and make it easy to say yes to.

If you want to build this sequence for your target sectors, book a free cold email strategy call with the CleanReach team. We'll map out a sequence based on your local market and the types of contracts you want to win.

videoScripts.hook: Facilities managers aren't ghosting you — your email just gave them no reason to reply.
videoScripts.problem: Here's the thing about facilities managers — they're not hard to reach. They're just impossible to impress with a pitch that sounds like it was copy-pasted from a template. And most cleaning company emails? That's exactly what they are.
videoScripts.solution: Try this instead. Send three emails, spaced a few days apart. The first one shows you understand their specific environment — whether that's a school, a healthcare site, or a busy office block. The second one gets practical — mention the scale of the building and what a realistic cleaning schedule would look like. The third is just a nudge. Short. Friendly. Makes it easy for them to say yes to a quick call or a site visit. No hard sell. No attachments. Just specificity.
videoScripts.proof: This kind of sequence works because it treats facilities managers like professionals, not just a name on a list. Companies using it are getting 3 to 5 times more replies than with a generic pitch.
videoScripts.cta: Want the full sequence? Book a free cold email strategy call — link in my bio. It's 20 minutes and I'll show you exactly how to set this up for your area.
